questions.text: The nucleus of an atom consists of:
questions.explanation: Protons and neutrons (collectively called nucleons) reside in the nucleus.

questions.text: The radius R of a nucleus with mass number A is given by:
questions.explanation: The radius is proportional to the cube root of the mass number.

questions.text: The density of nuclear matter is:
questions.explanation: Volume ∝ A and Mass ∝ A, so Density = Mass/Volume is constant (approx 2.3 x 10¹⁷ kg/m³).

questions.text: Atoms having same atomic number but different mass numbers are called:
questions.explanation: Isotopes have same Z (number of protons) but different N (number of neutrons).

questions.text: Mass number of a nucleus is the total number of:
questions.explanation: A = Z + N.

questions.text: 1 atomic mass unit (amu) is equivalent to energy of approximately:
questions.explanation: Using E=mc², 1 amu ≈ 931.5 MeV.

questions.text: The difference between the rest mass of a nucleus and the sum of masses of its nucleons is:
questions.explanation: Mass defect Δm = [Z mp + (A-Z) mn] - M_nucleus.

questions.text: Which of the following determines the stability of a nucleus?
questions.explanation: Nuclei with higher BE/A are more stable.

questions.text: The nuclear force is:
questions.explanation: Nuclear force is a strong, short-range (few fm) attractive force that binds nucleons.

questions.text: In a stable nucleus, the number of neutrons compared to protons:
questions.explanation: Heavier nuclei need more neutrons to provide attractive nuclear force to overcome proton-proton repulsion.

questions.text: Radioactivity is a process that is:
questions.explanation: Radioactivity involves the spontaneous decay of unstable nuclei.

questions.text: The SI unit of radioactivity is:
questions.explanation: 1 Becquerel (Bq) = 1 decay per second.

questions.text: The half-life (T₁/₂) and decay constant (λ) are related as:
In alpha decay, the mass number A and atomic number Z change as:
questions.explanation: An alpha particle is a Helium nucleus (⁴He₂).

questions.text: In beta-minus decay, what is emitted along with an electron?
questions.explanation: An antineutrino is emitted to conserve lepton number and energy.

questions.text: Gamma rays are emitted when:
options: Nucleus jumps from excited state to ground state
questions.explanation: Similar to atoms, nuclei have energy levels; gamma photons are emitted during de-excitation.

questions.text: Nuclear fission is the process where:
questions.explanation: A heavy nucleus (like U-235) splits into smaller fragments, releasing energy.

questions.text: The source of energy in stars is:
questions.explanation: Stars fuse Hydrogen into Helium at extremely high temperatures.

questions.text: Controlled nuclear fission is used in:
questions.explanation: Nuclear reactors use control rods to maintain a steady chain reaction.

questions.text: Moderator in a nuclear reactor is used to:
questions.explanation: Slow (thermal) neutrons are more effective at causing fission in U-235.

questions.text: Heavy water (D₂O) is commonly used as a:
questions.explanation: Heavy water slows down neutrons without capturing them.

questions.text: Binding energy per nucleon is maximum for:
questions.explanation: Iron-56 has BE/A ≈ 8.8 MeV, making it the most stable nucleus.

questions.text: Nuclear fusion requires extremely high temperatures to:
questions.explanation: Positive nuclei must come close enough for nuclear force to act, which requires overcoming strong repulsion.

questions.text: If a sample has 1000 atoms, how many remain after two half-lives?
questions.explanation: 1000 → 500 (1st half-life) → 250 (2nd half-life).

questions.text: Which radiation has the highest ionizing power?
questions.explanation: Alpha particles are heavy and have +2 charge, interacting strongly with matter.

questions.text: Which radiation has the highest penetrating power?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are neutral and high energy, allowing them to pass through thick materials.

questions.text: The average life (τ) of a radioactive substance is:
questions.explanation: Average life τ = 1/λ ≈ 1.44 T₁/₂.

questions.text: Nuclear forces are NOT:
questions.explanation: Nuclear forces are non-central forces.

questions.text: Fusion of four protons into one Helium nucleus releases energy because:
options: He mass < sum of 4 H masses
He mass > sum of 4 H masses
questions.explanation: The mass difference (mass defect) is converted to energy via E=Δmc².

questions.text: Isotones are nuclei with same number of:
questions.explanation: Isotones have same (A - Z).

questions.text: Mass of a neutron is approximately:
questions.explanation: Neutron (1.00866 u) is slightly heavier than proton (1.00727 u). Both are ~1840x electron.

questions.text: The fraction of radioactive atoms remaining after time 't' is:
questions.explanation: From N = N₀ e^(-λt), the fraction N/N₀ = e^(-λt).

questions.text: Cadmium rods in a nuclear reactor act as:
questions.explanation: Cadmium is a good neutron absorber, used to control the fission rate.

questions.text: In beta-plus decay, a proton converts into:
questions.explanation: p → n + e⁺ + ν.

questions.text: Nuclear force saturation means:
options: A nucleon interacts only with its immediate neighbors
It is zero at large distances
questions.explanation: This explains why BE/A is constant for most nuclei.

questions.text: The curve of BE/A vs A drops at very large A due to:
questions.explanation: In large nuclei, proton repulsion starts to overcome the short-range nuclear attraction.

questions.text: Mass energy equivalence E = mc² was proposed by:
questions.explanation: This is the most famous equation from Einstein's Special Relativity.

questions.text: Atomic mass is measured using:
questions.explanation: Mass spectrographs use magnetic and electric fields to separate ions by mass.

questions.text: In the reaction: n + ²³⁵U → ¹⁴⁴Ba + ⁸⁹Kr + 3n, how many neutrons are produced per fission?
questions.explanation: Typically 2-3 neutrons are released, enabling a chain reaction.

questions.text: Which nucleus is the most difficult to break apart?
questions.explanation: Highest BE/A means it is most tightly bound and stable.
